ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC represents grilled chicken meat as a closest parent; no specific half/full portion code exists in FoodEx2.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is half a grilled chicken, approximately 400 g, as served at Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05013)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values adapted from USDA SR Legacy roasted chicken (meat and skin) as the closest approved source for Indian-style grilled whole chicken. Indian spice marinade contributes negligible macronutrient change per 100 g. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect typical spiced marinade contribution (~350 mg/100 g) consistent with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods reference ranges for marinated grilled chicken. ICMR-NIN (2017) Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, National Institute of Nutrition, Hyderabad, consulted for contextual validation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Grilled chicken is a protein-dominant food with negligible carbohydrate content (~0.5 g/100 g). Per Veritas v2 critical rule for zero-GI foods, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value, effectively zero) as this food contains no glycemic carbohydrates. gi_category is 'low' per convention. glycemic_load_per_serving = 0.0 confirmed.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from spiced marinade (~350 mg/100 g; ~1400 mg per half-chicken serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of portion size, as a half-chicken serving contributes approximately 1400 mg sodium — over 60% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — and should request reduced-salt marinade where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content (0.5 g/100 g) and high protein (25 g/100 g) with no glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled chicken is an excellent protein choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate content and low glycemic load, supporting blood glucose stability when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as naan or rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (3.5 g/100 g) and cholesterol (88 mg/100 g) from skin-on preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer skinless grilled chicken to reduce saturated fat and cholesterol intake, as the skin contributes the majority of the saturated fat content; the lean meat itself is a heart-healthy protein source rich in B vitamins and selenium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Chicken (Half or Full) is a popular Indian-cuisine dish served at Indian restaurants in Singapore, including establishments such as Anjana Kitchen. The chicken — typically a whole broiler — is marinated in a spiced yogurt-based mixture containing garlic, ginger, chilli, turmeric, cumin, and coriander before being grilled over high heat or in a tandoor-style oven. The result is a juicy, smoky, and aromatic dish with a charred exterior and tender interior. It is available in half-portion (~400 g) or full-portion (~800 g) sizes. Nutritionally, grilled chicken is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food providing approximately 215 kcal, 25 g protein, and 12.5 g fat per 100 g. The fat profile includes beneficial monounsaturated fats alongside saturated fat primarily from the skin. Sodium content is elevated due to the spiced marinade (~350 mg/100 g). The dish has an effectively zero glycemic index (set to 1) and low glycemic load, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. It is naturally gluten-free and typically halal-certified in Singapore Indian restaurants. Clinically, it is a strong protein source for muscle maintenance, weight management, and diabetic meal planning, though sodium and saturated fat content warrant attention for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. Rich in niacin, vitamin B6, selenium, and phosphorus.
common_user_queries: How many calories in half a grilled chicken?
Is Indian grilled chicken good for diabetics?
What is the protein content of grilled chicken?
Is grilled chicken halal in Singapore?
Can I eat grilled chicken with high blood pressure?
Grilled chicken vs fried chicken nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values adapted from USDA SR Legacy roasted chicken (meat and skin) as the closest approved source; no exact ICMR-NIN or HPB entry for Indian-style grilled whole chicken was identified. Sodium adjusted to reflect typical spiced marinade contribution. Confidence is medium rather than high because the source match is adapted, not exact.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 for grilled chicken meat; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — chicken muscle tissue contains no dietary fibre. 2. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for cooked chicken meat. 3. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) per Veritas v2 critical rule for non-glycemic foods; gi_category is 'low' per University of Sydney GI Database convention for non-glycemic foods. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate_g per serving is negligible. 4. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g/100 g reflects naturally occurring trans fats (vaccenic acid) in poultry, not industrial trans fats. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all sourced from USDA SR Legacy NDB 05013 (roasted chicken, meat and skin). 6. Serving size of 400 g (half chicken) is an estimate based on typical restaurant portion; actual weight may vary. 7. Halal status assumed based on Indian Muslim restaurant context (Anjana Kitchen); not independently verified. 8. GI source match type is analogous_estimate as no direct GI measurement for Indian-style grilled whole chicken was found in the University of Sydney GI Database; the convention of setting GI to 1 for negligible-carbohydrate protein foods is established in peer-reviewed GI literature (Atkinson et al. 2021, AJCN) and aligns with Veritas v2 critical rules.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected glycemic_index from 0 to 1 to comply with Veritas v2 critical rule for zero-GI foods, which requires a numeric value greater than zero. Updated gi_source.source_match_notes and general notes to reflect this change and justification.
